pages.text:
Squats are a compound exercise that primarily targets
the quadriceps, hamstrings, and glutes. The exercise
is performed using body weight or a barbell placed
across the upper back. Proper form requires keeping
the chest up and the lower back neutral throughout
the movement. The squat begins from a standing
position, descends until the hips are below the
knees, and returns to standing.
Deadlifts are another fundamental compound movement
that engages the entire posterior chain including the
erector spinae, glutes, and hamstrings. The
conventional deadlift uses a barbell and begins with
the weight on the floor. The lifter hinges at the
hips while maintaining a flat back, grips the bar
with both hands, and stands up by extending the hips
and knees simultaneously.
The bench press is an upper body exercise that
targets the pectoralis major, anterior deltoids, and
triceps brachii. It is performed lying on a flat
bench while pressing a barbell from the chest to full
arm extension. The leg press machine provides an
alternative to squats for those with lower back
issues, isolating the quadriceps while reducing
spinal loading.
For core stability, planks and Russian twists are
recommended. Planks engage the rectus abdominis and
transverse abdominis without equipment. Russian
twists additionally target the obliques and can be
weighted with a medicine ball for progression.
